questions.question_text: All Ohio real estate advertising must include which identifying information?
distractors: Only the salesperson's name
The agent's license number
The Division's phone number
questions.explanation: Ohio law requires all advertising to include the brokerage name as registered with the Division. While a salesperson's name may appear, the brokerage name is mandatory.

questions.question_text: An Ohio salesperson posts a property listing on social media using only their personal name and photo, without mentioning their brokerage. This is:
questions.correct_answer: A blind ad, which is prohibited
distractors: Acceptable on personal social media accounts
Permitted if the agent's license is visible
Allowed if the post is not paid advertising
questions.explanation: Advertising without brokerage identification is a blind ad, prohibited under Ohio law. Social media advertising follows the same rules — the brokerage name must appear.

questions.question_text: A real estate team in Ohio creates advertising under their team name 'Dream Home Team.' What additional element must appear?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage name must also be included
distractors: No additional element is required
The team leader's license number
A Division-approved team registration number
questions.explanation: Team names may be used in Ohio advertising, but the brokerage name must always appear as well. The team name alone does not satisfy the advertising requirement.

questions.question_text: Which advertising standard applies equally to print, internet, and social media advertising in Ohio?
questions.correct_answer: Brokerage name must be included in all formats
distractors: Internet ads are exempt from brokerage name rules
Social media only requires agent name disclosure
Print advertising has stricter requirements
questions.explanation: Ohio's advertising rules apply uniformly across all media — print, internet, and social media. The brokerage name must be included regardless of the advertising platform.

questions.question_text: An Ohio brokerage changes its registered name but continues using the old name in existing online ads. This is:
questions.correct_answer: A violation until ads are updated
distractors: Acceptable for up to 90 days after the change
Permitted if the old name redirects to the new one
Only a violation for print advertising
questions.explanation: All advertising must use the brokerage name as currently registered with the Division. Continuing to use an old name in any advertising format is a violation.

questions.question_text: An Ohio agent tells a prospective buyer that a home's value will double in two years to encourage a purchase. This is an example of:
questions.correct_answer: A false promise likely to influence a party
distractors: Legitimate puffing about property value
An acceptable marketing statement
A disclosure of material investment facts
questions.explanation: Making false promises likely to influence someone to buy, sell, or lease property is a specific ground for discipline under ORC 4735.18. Guaranteed appreciation claims are prohibited.

questions.question_text: What is the distinction between material misrepresentation and inadvertent error under Ohio law?
questions.correct_answer: It affects the severity of the penalty imposed
distractors: Only intentional fraud is subject to discipline
Inadvertent errors are never grounds for discipline
Both receive identical penalties under ORC 4735
questions.explanation: Ohio recognizes a distinction between deliberate material misrepresentation and inadvertent error. Both may be grounds for discipline, but the penalty severity differs based on intent.

questions.question_text: A licensed salesperson tells a buyer they are a licensed broker. This violates which Ohio provision?
distractors: The Canon of Ethics provision only
The advertising brokerage name rule
The trust account regulation rules
questions.explanation: Falsely representing license status (such as claiming to be a broker when licensed as a salesperson) is specifically prohibited under Ohio license law.

questions.question_text: An agent knowingly tells a buyer that a property has no foundation issues despite being aware of significant cracks. This is:
distractors: Acceptable if the seller authorized the statement
Puffing, which is generally permitted
Only actionable if the buyer suffers financial loss
questions.explanation: Knowingly making false statements about property condition is material misrepresentation, a specific ground for discipline under ORC 4735.18 regardless of seller authorization.

questions.question_text: A licensee falsely claims to hold a Certified Residential Specialist designation they do not have. This violates:
questions.correct_answer: The prohibition on false claims of expertise
distractors: Only the local Realtor association rules
Federal advertising standards only
No Ohio law, as designations are voluntary
questions.explanation: Ohio prohibits licensees from falsely claiming specialized expertise or designations they do not hold. This protects consumers from misleading credentials.

questions.question_text: An Ohio licensee wants to purchase a property for their own investment. Before making an offer, they must:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose their license status in writing
distractors: Get approval from the Division of Real Estate
Notify the Ohio Real Estate Commission
Have their broker make the offer on their behalf
questions.explanation: When buying, selling, or leasing property for their own account, Ohio licensees must disclose their license status in writing before any offer is presented.

questions.question_text: An Ohio agent lists their own home for sale through their brokerage. When must the license disclosure be made to potential buyers?
distractors: At the time of closing only
Within 3 days of showing the property
Only if the buyer asks directly
questions.explanation: The written disclosure of license status must be made before any offer is presented. This ensures buyers know they are dealing with a licensed professional who has industry knowledge.

questions.question_text: A licensee is leasing their investment property directly, without using an agent. Must they still disclose their license status?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, disclosure is required for their own leasing
distractors: No, only sales transactions require disclosure
Only if the lease exceeds 12 months
Only if they are managing the property professionally
questions.explanation: Ohio requires license disclosure when a licensee buys, sells, or leases property for their own account. This applies to all transaction types, including leasing.

questions.question_text: An Ohio licensee purchases property at a foreclosure auction for personal investment. They did not disclose their license status. This is:
questions.correct_answer: A violation of Ohio disclosure requirements
distractors: Acceptable since foreclosure auctions are public
Permitted for investment properties only
A violation only if they resell within 6 months
questions.explanation: License disclosure is required whenever a licensee deals in property for their own account, regardless of the transaction type. Foreclosure auction purchases are not exempt.

questions.question_text: The purpose of requiring licensees to disclose their status when dealing with their own property is to:
questions.correct_answer: Alert others they have professional knowledge
distractors: Collect additional licensing fees from the Division
Ensure the transaction is recorded with the Commission
Prevent licensees from owning investment property
questions.explanation: The disclosure requirement ensures that parties know they are dealing with someone who has specialized real estate knowledge, which could provide an advantage in negotiations.
